stage_description: Stage 4 — Market of Skeptics. Prospects have been exposed to every type of golf ball claim: distance (everyone claims it), feel (everyone claims it), value (DTC brands claim it), technology (dimples, cores, covers). They're skeptical of claims and focus on proof, specificity, and mechanism differentiation.

evidence_for_stage_4.signal: Prospects compare claims across brands with skepticism
evidence_for_stage_4.evidence: Golfers test balls side-by-side on launch monitors, compare pricing, share data. They don't trust marketing — they trust their own experience and peer reviews.
prospect_language: I bought a launch monitor and it shows I lose 10 yards on drives with ProV1
I did a blind test between the 2 on my sim
I've been experimenting this year, using Bridgestone e12 & Callaway super soft

evidence_for_stage_4.signal: Price sensitivity has shifted from 'can I afford it' to 'is it worth it'
evidence_for_stage_4.evidence: Golfers don't say 'I can't afford Pro V1.' They say 'Pro V1 isn't WORTH what they charge.' This is Stage 4 — they've bought premium and now question whether the premium delivered.
prospect_language: The difference in performance vs. cost isn't worth it
The entire golf ball industry is a scam. They all cost close to the same to make
Nearly the same performance, half the price

evidence_for_stage_4.signal: DTC brands have already made the 'same ball for less' argument
evidence_for_stage_4.evidence: Vice, Snell, Maxfli, and Kirkland have already positioned as 'premium performance at lower prices.' This argument has been made. The market has absorbed it. A new entrant can't lead with 'same quality for less' — that's Stage 3 positioning in a Stage 4 market.
prospect_language: Instead of ProV1 at $55, play Vice at $26. Nearly the same performance
Maxfli Tour X feels and reacts no different than a ProV1x
I tried so hard to be a Snell and Vice guy

evidence_for_stage_4.signal: Golfers cite specific data points, not vague impressions
evidence_for_stage_4.evidence: Discussions include compression numbers, launch angles, spin rates, specific yardage differences. This is a data-literate market segment that demands specificity.
prospect_language: I was surprised that some of the highest compression balls performed the best for mid-swing speed
I find that at this ball speed I get the best results launching in the 13-14° range with 2350 spin
Definitely robbing yourself of 25+ yards by being at -2 angle of attack

why_not_stage_5: Stage 5 (market of cynics) would mean prospects don't believe ANY golf ball claim. That's not the case — golfers still get excited about testing new balls, they still believe the right ball can help their game. They're skeptical of MARKETING claims but open to DATA-backed claims. That's the key distinction.

lead_strategy_for_stage_4.primary: Lead with MECHANISM + PROOF, not with promise or claim
lead_strategy_for_stage_4.rationale: In Stage 4, the market has heard every promise. 'More distance' is meaningless. 'Engineered for 80-95mph swing speeds, independently tested at Golf Laboratories, beat Pro V1 in carry distance at 95mph' is mechanism + proof. The SPECIFICITY of the claim is what breaks through skepticism.
recommended_approach: Lead with the swing-speed matching mechanism — 'this ball is designed for YOUR swing speed, not a tour pro's'
Anchor every claim to Golf Laboratories robot data — independent, third-party, inarguable
Use SPECIFIC numbers (239.3 yards, not 'farther'; 95mph, not 'moderate swing speed')
Let the data do the convincing — the Soul.md voice ('knowledgeable golf buddy') is perfect for Stage 4 because it presents evidence without hype
The visual alignment tech is the DIFFERENTIATOR that no competitor has — this is the Stage 4 'unique mechanism' play

saturated_claims: Soft feel (every mid-tier ball claims this)
Tour-quality performance (meaningless — tours play different balls than amateurs need)
Value/savings vs. premium (Vice, Maxfli, Kirkland all own this)
unsaturated_claims: Swing-speed optimized engineering (no ball is SPECIFICALLY positioned for 80-95mph as a primary identity)
Visual alignment technology (Triple Track exists but no one owns 'alignment system' as a category)
Independent robot testing as the proof standard (most brands cite their own testing, not third-party)
Recovery of 'lost' distance through ball optimization (no one frames ball choice as recovering yards you thought were gone)
